title: Looking at your younger self might help you recall childhood memories more vividly
html_body:
What matters in science |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 15 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
Today we use thermal imaging to see night-flying birds, investigate issues with some AI disease-prediction models and discover that looking at your younger self might help you recall childhood memories more vividly.

If you enjoy this newsletter, please consider recommending it to a friend or colleague. Click here to forward it by e-mail. Thank you!
To capture images of birds, such as this yellow-rumped warbler (Setophaga coronata), flying at night, one research-team member detected the thermal signature of the bird and shone a torch at it. Another team member then took a snap with a digital camera. (Ross Gallardy/Macaulay Library)

‘Invisible’ birds are brought to the light

Researchers have used thermal imaging technology to reveal and track the movements of bird species that travel at night. Some birds complete legs of their migrations at night because the air is cooler than during the day, and possibly to make it more difficult for predators to see them. But their nocturnal activities have long stumped ornithologists wanting to catalogue the birds’ movements. The ability to visualize night-flying birds could help reveal which species are most vulnerable to threats such as wind turbines and light pollution, says ecologist Felix Liechti.

Science | 6 min read
 Reference: Ornithology paper

Some disease-predictors use dodgy data

Dozens of artificial-intelligence models designed to predict a person’s risk of stroke or diabetes are trained on dubious datasets. Researchers found that many such models used one of two open-access health datasets that provide little information about where the data come from, and contain multiple oddities that suggest the data could have been fabricated. At least two of these models have been used in hospitals in Indonesia and Spain, although it’s unclear whether this has led to any flawed diagnoses.

Nature | 7 min read
 Reference: medRxiv preprint (not peer reviewed)

NSF fellowships in surprise funding surge

The US National Science Foundation (NSF) has announced a record number — 2,599 — of prestigious Graduate Research Fellowship Program awards. The increase came as a surprise after the number of awards was cut in half last year, this year’s application window opened late and some submissions were rejected without peer review. Quantum science and AI — priorities of the administration of US President Donald Trump — were the big winners this year, but biological sciences also got an unexpected boost.

Nature | 7 min read

Features & opinion

More fossil fuels won’t fix the energy crisis

Renewable energy sources are the best way to stymie the rising costs of fossil fuels driven by conflicts such as the ongoing war in Iran, argues climate economist Gernot Wagner. Abandoning fossil fuels could cause temporary ‘greenflation’ — price hikes for tech such as solar panels in the face of increased demand — but the solution is to accelerate the transition away from fossil fuels by producing more low-carbon technologies, Wagner writes. “Shifting to technologies that can only get cheaper and better over time is an investment in geopolitical and price stability.”

Nature | 7 min read

Taxonomy data is a must for AI systems

The disappearance of formal taxonomy training at undergraduate level could hobble the development of medical and agricultural artificial-intelligence systems that rely on biological data, argues systems biologist Chris Bivins. Without formal, scientifically meaningful classification for organisms such as fungi, of which only 10% have been described, they don’t appear in the literature on which large language models are trained. That could result in AI systems that can’t distinguish between organisms that are benign and dangerous to humans, with potentially harmful consequences in fields such as drug development, Bivins writes.

Nature | 5 min read

Youthifying ‘mirror’ brings back memories

People who look at a mirror-like image of a childlike version of their own face can better recall early memories, write cognitive neuroscientists Jane Aspell and Utkarsh Gupta. People who saw their ‘younger face’ recalled significantly “richer, more vivid details” of childhood memories than those who viewed
their unaltered appearance, say the researchers. The results suggest “that the bodily self isn’t just a backdrop but instead is foundational to how memories are encoded and organized in the brain”.

Scientific American | 8 min read
 Reference: Scientific Reports paper

Quote of the day

“Adaptability is less a virtue than it is a requirement. For immigrant scientists from the global south, it is often the price of participation.”

Biologist Mayank Chugh was denied a visa to enter South Africa just two weeks before a field trip with 15 undergraduate students. The experience “exposed the reality of visa privilege in academia”, he says. His students — all US citizens — didn’t require a visa to enter South Africa. But he, only having an Indian passport, did. (Nature | 6 min read)

On Saturday, the beloved science-sidekick C. elegans was launched on its very own space mission in its very own space pod. The nematode worms are housed inside a miniature laboratory dubbed the Petri Pod, which will be mounted on the outside of the International Space Station to help understand the effects of deep-space journeys. It’s not the first time that the tiny worms have slipped the surly bonds of Earth: they’ve helped measure muscle loss over multiple generations on the Space Station and some even survived the tragic destruction of the Space Shuttle Columbia.

Help this newsletter to thrive in your inbox by sending us your feedback — whether positive or critical — to briefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Flora Graham, senior editor, Nature Briefing
With contributions by Jacob Smith

items.title: ‘Invisible’ birds are brought to the light
items.snippet: Researchers have used thermal imaging technology to reveal and track the movements of bird species that travel at night . items.title: Some disease-predictors use dodgy data
items.snippet: Dozens of artificial-intelligence models designed to predict a person’s risk of stroke or diabetes are trained on dubious datasets. Researchers found that many such models used one of two open-access health datasets that provide little information about where the data come from, and contain multiple oddities that suggest the data could have been fabricated . At least two of these models have been used in hospitals in Indonesia and Spain, although it’s unclear whether this has led to any flawed diagnoses.

items.title: NSF fellowships in surprise funding surge
items.snippet: The US National Science Foundation (NSF) has announced a record number — 2,599 — of prestigious Graduate Research Fellowship Program awards. The increase came as a surprise after the number of awards was cut in half last year, this year’s application window opened late and some submissions were rejected without peer review. Quantum science and AI — priorities of the administration of US President Donald Trump — were the big winners this year, but biological sciences also got an unexpected boost.

Abandoning fossil fuels could cause temporary ‘greenflation’ — price hikes for tech such as solar panels in the face of increased demand — but the solution is to accelerate the transition away from fossil fuels by producing more low-carbon technologies , Wagner writes. “Shifting to technologies that can only get cheaper and better over time is an investment in geopolitical and price stability.”

summary.overview: 이번 과학 뉴스레터에서는 어린 시절의 자신을 바라보는 것이 어린 시절의 기억을 더 선명하게 되살릴 수 있다는 주제로, 주요 기사 6편을 다룹니다. 각 기사는 생물학, 인공지능, 에너지 정책, 데이터 윤리 등 다양한 분야의 최신 연구를 소개합니다.
covered_item_titles: ‘Invisible’ birds are brought to the light
NSF fellowships in surprise funding surge
More fossil fuels won’t fix the energy crisis
Taxonomy data is a must for AI systems

article_briefings.title: ‘Invisible’ birds are brought to the light
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 열화상 기술을 활용해 야간에 이동하는 '보이지 않는' 새 종을 추적하는 연구가 발표됐습니다. 이 새들은 낮보다 더 시원한 밤공기를 이용해 이동하며, 이 발견은 생태학적 이동 패턴을 이해하는 데 기여할 수 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: Some disease-predictors use dodgy data
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 뇌졸중이나 당뇨병 위험을 예측하는 인공지능 모델이 불완전한 공개 데이터셋을 기반으로 개발됐다는 연구 결과가 공개됐습니다. 이는 의료 AI의 신뢰성에 대한 우려를 제기합니다.

article_briefings.title: NSF fellowships in surprise funding surge
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 미국 국립과학기초연구소(NSF)가 기 record 수준인 2,599명의 박사후 연구원에게 장학금을 지급했습니다. 이는 지난해 수여량이 반으로 줄어든 후의 급증으로, 과학 인재 양성에 대한 투자 확대를 반영합니다.

article_briefings.title: More fossil fuels won’t fix the energy crisis
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 에너지 위기 해결을 위해 화석 연료 의존도를 높이는 것은 효과적이지 않으며, 재생에너지 확대가 필수적이라고 경제학자들이 주장했습니다. 단기적 '그린 인플레이션' 가능성도 경고했습니다.

article_briefings.title: Taxonomy data is a must for AI systems
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 생물 분류학(타세우학) 교육이 대학 수준에서 줄어들고 있는 것이 의료 및 농업 AI 개발에 부정적 영향을 줄 수 있다고 생물학자가 경고했습니다. 정확한 분류 데이터가 AI의 신뢰성에 필수적이라고 강조했습니다.

article_briefings.title: Youthifying ‘mirror’ brings back memories
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 자신의 어린 시절 모습을 거울에 비추는 것이 어린 시절의 기억을 더 선명하게 되살릴 수 있다는 연구 결과가 나왔습니다. 이는 정서적 기억 회복과 관련된 신경과학적 통찰을 제공합니다.

raw_metadata.subject: Looking at your younger self might help you recall childhood memories more vividly
